title.title: Whose Pen Wrote the Map? Battling Over the Armenian Medieval Text Ashkharhatsuyts with Stylometry

author.affiliation: École nationale des chartes - Université PSL, France
École nationale des chartes - Université PSL, France

sourceDesc.p: Converted from an OASIS Open Document

keywords.term: I plan to attend the conference on-site in Lisbon

div.head: Introduction
div.p: The Armenian geographical treatise Ashkharhatsuyts (Աշխարհացոյց) is as a distinctive source of information on the geography and history of the Caucasus and the Sassanid Empire prior to the Arab conquests, and includes an extensive section describing Europe, Africa, and Asia, which appears to draw influence from the Geography of Pappus of Alexandria (Hewsen 1971) or other Oriental sources derivated from it (Cowe 1997).

Its authorship has been a topic of scholarly debate. Initially, the work was ascribed to Movses Khorenatsi, a 5th-century historian. This attribution was challenged in the late 19th century, with Anania Shirakatsi, a 7th-century scholar, proposed as the more probable author (Hewsen and others 1992). Support for Shirakatsi’s authorship is grounded in several arguments. The Ashkharhatsuyts references geopolitical entities and events pertinent to the early 7th century, aligning with Anania Shirakatsi’s era. Additionally, the treatise exhibits scientific methodologies and stylistic elements characteristic of Shirakatsi’s known works, particularly in its systematic approach to geographical description and incorporation of astronomical knowledge, Anania being mainly known for his works in mathematics (notably weights and measures), as well as for his treatise on cosmology. Furthermore, early manuscripts of the Ashkharhatsuyts do not explicitly attribute the work to Movses Khorenatsi, leaving room for alternative authorship theories. Conversely, arguments favoring Khorenatsi’s authorship include historical tradition and some medieval sources that have linked the Ashkharhatsuyts to Movses Khorenatsi, reflecting a longstanding acceptance of his authorship. Proponents also contend that specific narrative elements and literary styles within the text exhibit similarities to Khorenatsi’s historiographical approach. Furthermore, the ongoing debate regarding the precise period of Khorenatsi’s activity (some attribute him to the 7th century, T’op’ch’yan 2006)) allows for the possibility of his timeline aligning with the composition date of Ashkharhatsuyts.

Although the prevailing scholarly consensus attributes the Ashkharhatsuyts to Anania Shirakatsi, no comprehensive stylometric analysis has been conducted until now, leaving the debate open. In fact, as far as we are aware, very little computational analysis of classical Armenian texts has ever been performed, if any. Proponents of Movses Khorenatsi’s authorship continue to cite traditional attribution and literary parallels, highlighting the complexity of resolving this historiographical question.

div.head: Materials and Methods
div.p: To address this question, we constructed a corpus (Table 1) comprising texts from the two candidates, as well as two contemporaneous authors included as distractors: Eghishe, a 5th-6th-centuries author renowned for his historical work History of Vardan and various exegetical writings, and Ghazar Parpetsi, a historiographer active during the 5th centuries. For each author, we selected all texts authenticated in the Matenabgirk’ Hayoc’ (Collective, n.d.), with the exclusion of those subject to attribution debates. We used either the digitised versions available on Digilib, or employed OCR technology using the Calfa software for Armenian. All texts were standardised to ensure consistency, which included the removal of editorial annotations, of intonation marks, of punctuation, and of titles introduced by editors, and standardisation of ligatured forms (և եւ) and of the classical orthography between editions (e.g. թե թէ).
Table 1. Corpus of texts used for authorship analysis, with the two candidates (Movses Khorenatsi and Anania Shirakatsi) and two distractors (Eghishe and Ghazar Parpetsi). From Matenagirk’ Hayoc’.

row.cell: History of Rhipsime and Her Companions
On the Manifestation of Our Lord

div.p: This corpus is relatively imbalanced. In particular, the two main candidates are problematic: only another single text is attributed to Movses with certainty ( History of Armenia), while two others ( History of Rhipsime and Her Companion, Letter to Sahag) are usually attributed to him, without an absolute consensus because of uncertainties related to their date (see Appendix, Figure 4). Although there are 8 texts for Anania, most of them are very short (even below 1000 words). To compensate for the imbalance, we use a sampling technique: we take 10,000 samples of 1,000 word tokens for each text, by using random sampling with replacement; then, we randomly select among them 10,000 total samples by author. We compare this method to selecting slices of 1,000 consecutive words in the texts, a method more intuitive but probably less efficient (Eder 2015).

On each sample, we compute the frequencies of the function words, idiolectal features that were chosen to avoid thematic attractions. From there, we train linear SVM models. For cross-validation, we use group-k-fold cross-validation, a very demanding choice for the model, but potentially more informative, as all the samples from each given text are iteratively removed from train and used as validation. This avoids measuring the ability of the model to detect parts of the same text, but instead measures its capacity to identify authorship. Current scores are shown in Table 2.

Overall accuracy (0.61 with sampling) remains relatively low, and performance is unequal between candidates, reflecting the difficulty of the task and the limited amount of training material. In the detailed analysis of attribution errors, frequent use of consecrated liturgical formulas, such as “now and forever,” emerges as a factor contributing to stylistic overlaps between authors and reducing distinctiveness. Last but not least, some uncertainties cannot be completely ruled out regarding the actual attribution of some of the training material (see below).
Table 2. SVM training scores, using group-k-fold cross validation, 1000 words samples, on subsequent (left) or random (right) samples

The coefficients for the two primary candidates (Figure 1) reveal significant differences in linguistic habits. According to our corpus, Anania Shirakatsi predominantly employs temporal adverbs such as արդ (/ard/, now) and յորժամ (/yoržam/, when), conjuctive adverbs like այսպէս (/aysbēs/, so) and նոյնպէս (/noynbēs/, likewise), and markers for subordinate clauses. In contrast, Movses Khorenatsi demonstrates a preference for a diverse range of common pre/postpositions (ի /i/, յետ /yet/) and personal or anaphoric pronouns in the genitive (նորա /nora/, սորա /sora/). This divergence highlights stylistic influences: Anania’s language likely reflects the syntactic and grammatical patterns characteristic of the Hellenizing school, likely influenced by his reliance on Greek sources (Muradyan 2014). In contrast, Movses’ style aligns more closely with traditional Armenian sentence constructions. The use of pronouns in Movses Khorenatsi’s works also reflects his predilection for historiography, with its focus on narrative accounts and the frequent referencing of individuals or events. This distinguishes his style from that of Anania Shirakatsi, whose works are often influenced by translated philosophocal or scientific texts.

p.hi: Coefficients of the models for the two main candidates

div.head: Results and discussion
div.p: Current results (Figure 2 and appendix, Figure 4) consistently attribute the Ashkharhatsuyts to Anania Shirakatsi, regardless of the sampling method used (sequential or random). However, there is some uncertainty surrounding the attribution of the first 1000 words, which appear likely to belong to Anania but are also contested by the distractors. This segment corresponds to Book 1 and the introduction to Book 2 of the Ashkharhatsuyts, while the remainder of the text focuses on descriptions of Europe, Africa, and Asia. These latter sections, influenced by the Geography of Pappus of Alexandria, are likely translations from Greek, aligning with Anania’s known reliance on Hellenizing sources. This distinction between the introductory and descriptive sections suggests the possibility of varying stylistic influences within the text.

p.hi: Results of the rolling SVM analysis on the target text, with sequential (top) and random (bottom) sampling – window 1000 words, step 100 words

div.p: In addition to the clear attribution of the Ashkharhatsuyts to Anania Shirakatsi, it is noteworthy that Movses Khorenatsi does not outperform one of the distractors, Ghazar Parpetsi. This outcome strongly suggests that Movses Khorenatsi can be definitively ruled out as a candidate for the authorship of this work. These results lead to two key hypotheses at this stage:

div.head: Attribution of the first 1000 words
div.p: While a style specific to Anania Shirakatsi cannot be excluded in these initial passages, it is equally plausible that they represent a compilation or adaptation of various texts distinct from the rest of the Ashkharhatsuyts. This possibility warrants further investigation into the stylistic and thematic coherence of these segments.

div.head: Attribution of the rest of the text
div.p: The strong attribution to Anania Shirakatsi from this point onward raises important questions. Given that these sections are inspired by Greek sources, and Anania’s corpus is characterized by translation work, this alignment may reflect a bias inherent in the dataset rather than a distinct stylistic signature. To address this, it would be necessary to include a distractor from the Hellenizing school for comparison, allowing for verification of whether Anania’s style is uniquely identifiable within this tradition.

div.head: Unexpected result on Eghishe, Commentary on Genesis:
div.p: In addition to the findings regarding the Ashkharhatsuyts, an unexpected result has emerged. During training, the SVM model consistently attributes the Commentary on Genesis, traditionally ascribed to Eghishe, to Anania Shirakatsi, with occasional attributions to Movses Khorenatsi (see Appendix, Figure 3). This raises intriguing questions about the authorship of this text and highlights the need for further investigation, between alternative possible attributions or the impact of the quotations of Genesis.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: Stylometric analysis of the Ashkharhatsuyts rules out Movses Khorenatsi and confirms a clear attribution to Anania Shirakatsi, consistent across sampling methods. However, the introductory passages exhibit stylistic ambiguities that may reflect compilations or external influences. The results suggest that the Greek-influenced sections align well with Anania’s known reliance on Hellenizing traditions but raise questions about potential biases in the dataset. Future research should incorporate additional Hellenizing distractors and refine methods to isolate distinctive stylistic features of Anania within this broader tradition.

div.head: Appendix
div.p: Table 3. Training scores without the dubious Commentary on Genesis
Figure 3. Rolling analysis of the Commentary on Genesis traditionally attributed to Eghishe
Figure 4. Main analysis, repeated with only the most certain text of Movses in training ( History of Armenia) (rolling SVM analysis, random sampling – window 1000 words, step 100 words)

div.note: Digital Library of Armenian Literature: https://digilib.aua.am

The analysis, when repeated with pronouns excluded, produced the same general attribution results for the target text. The training results were comparable but showed a slight decline in performance, indicating that while pronouns contribute to the model’s accuracy, their removal does not significantly alter the overall conclusions.

listBibl.head: Bibliography
listBibl.bibl: Cowe, S Peter. 1997. “The Geography of Ananias of Sirak (Ashkarhatsuyts), The Long and the Short Recensions: Introduction, Translation and Commentary.” The Journal of the American Oriental Society 117 (2): 379–81.
Eder, Maciej. 2015. “Does Size Matter? Authorship Attribution, Small Samples, Big Problem.” Digital Scholarship in the Humanities 30 (2): 167–82.
Hewsen, Robert H. 1971. “The geography of Pappus of Alexandria: a translation of the Armenian fragments.” Isis 62 (2): 186–207.
Hewsen, Robert H, and others. 1992. The geography of Ananias of Širak:(Ašxarhacʿoycʿ); the long and the short recensions. Reichert.
Muradyan, Gohar. 2014. “The Hellenizing School.” In Armenian Philology in the Modern Era, 321–48. Brill.
T’op’ch’yan, Aram. 2006. The Problem of the Greek Sources of Movsēs Xorenacʻi’s History of Armenia. Vol. HUAS 7. Peeters.
